text: Yeah.

text: You know, we'd either be working on getting ready to do an experiment, so we'd be working

text: Yeah.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Or setting up the experiment.

text: And, um, it all had to do with, uh, you know, the output of the accelerator and adjusting

text: that.

text: Oh, interesting.

text: Oh, cool.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And the beam splits off, it, uh, it comes down and it, it splits off into different experimental

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: areas.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: So you can conduct a whole bunch of different experiments, you know, just from one, one beam.

text: And they can put different things in the targets to make different particles and go different

text: directions.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And depending on the speed of the particle going by and its spin and how long it's in transit,

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: you can calculate its mass and, you know, backtrack and then put things together.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And, you know, analogy I always gave was, uh, it's, it's like if you wanted to analyze

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: a Swiss watch, but you really couldn't look inside.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: But so you could take it and throw it as hard as you can at a concrete wall and then it busts

text: Yeah.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: was made just from the particles flying apart.

text: So essentially what an accelerator does is smash something apart and then we can figure

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: out how it all goes together just by monitoring those particles.

text: It's so cool.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Yeah.

text: So, I mean, that's, that's, that's what I did at Los Alamos.

text: So they didn't go in depth, but in case your project connected to some of these in some way,

text: you had to have a brief overview of what else was going on.

text: Project Galileo was the first, uh, you know, the first briefing on there.

text: Okay.

text: Yeah.

text: glass and sidekick.

text: Is that right?

text: It's, uh, um, and directive two was to be able to remotely, um, disable the operation of the

text: system.

text: Uh, and some, I don't know how it was worded somewhere.

text: So, um, that was really a high priority, but they wanted to, uh, they certainly wanted to

text: duplicate it, but they really wanted to disable it.

text: I do think that's an important distinction in the mandate of the program.

text: Yeah.

text: Or come up with a hybrid system, something.

text: Yeah.

text: I mean, the, the bottom line is we want to produce the effects this machine is having.

text: So just do it in any, yeah, however, however you can just, yeah, just do it.

text: this outside of the box thinker.

text: Yeah.

text: uncover something.

text: but excuse me, sidekick dealt with using those to focus a particle beam to stop it from diverging.

text: that.

text: And, um.

text: Um, you know, there is stuff like, you know, the aliens had made 65 corrections to, um, you

text: Now, again, these claims don't lie in the realm of conventional proven science.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: But while there's no proverbial fire, there is a whole lot of smoke around them.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: I'm talking not only of the experiments of Townsend Brown, but of Eugene Pokletinov,

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Ning Li, and others.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And one single element might tie all of these stories together.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Bismuth.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: There's a reason why Bob Lazar kept hearing about it at S4.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Here's why bismuth matters.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: We'll break down the science as clearly as we can.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: It starts with something called a K-factor.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: A K-factor, or dielectric constant, is simply a material's ability to store and discharge

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: electric fields.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Now, this has important implications for historical anti-gravity experiments.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: You see, the higher the K-factor, the more thrust or propulsion you see in Townsend Brown's

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: capacitor experiments.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Brown spent his career searching for high K-materials that could amplify the effect he'd

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: discovered.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Bismuth is one of them, and it's often mentioned in the context of his anti-gravity work.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: There's even an interview from this guy, Lewis Witten, who's at RIAS, which is Martin

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Corporation, pre-Lockheed Merger, their anti-gravity outfit, where they were studying sort of the

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: most exotic propulsion modalities.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And he says in this interview with the American Institute of Physics, there's a guy named

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Townsend, who claimed to have an isotope of bismuth that repelled instead of attracted.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Material that works well for historical anti-gravity experiments comes up in the UFO reverse engineering

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: program.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Go figure.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: But it gets weirder.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Bismuth and element 115, muscovium, share the same number of valence electrons.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost shell of an atom, the ones that determine

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: how an element bonds, reacts, and behaves chemically.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Bismuth has five.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Muscovium, or element 115, has five.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: They sit in the same column of the periodic table, group 15, which means they have essentially

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: the same chemical personality, the same bonding geometry, the same family of crystal structures,

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: the same tendency to form the layered compounds that produce the most exotic quantum behavior

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: known to material science.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Lazar described element 115 as the fuel source for the craft's propulsion system.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Mind you, this was in 1989, before element 115 had ever been synthesized or named.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: When it finally was synthesized in 2003, it turned out to be anictogen, a group 15 element,

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: the same chemical family as bismuth.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And bismuth is basically the most electromagnetically bizarre stable element on Earth.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: That's either the most chemically literate lucky guess in history, or it isn't a guess

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: at all.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Now here's where the science gets genuinely strange.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Bismuth is one of the most unusual elements on the periodic table.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Most high-K materials are passive.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: They sit there, holding charge, and do nothing else.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Bismuth is different.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: It fights back.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Expose bismuth to a magnetic field, and instead of being attracted the way iron pushes towards

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: a magnet, it pushes away.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: This property is called diamagnetism, and bismuth has more of it than any other stable element

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: on Earth, not slightly more, dramatically, anomalously, inexplicably more.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: The reason lives inside the atom itself.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Every electron does two things simultaneously.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: It orbits the nucleus like a planet around a star, and it spins on its own axis like a

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: tiny top.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: In lighter elements, these two motions barely register each other.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: But bismuth sits near the bottom of the periodic table at element 83, one of the heaviest stable

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: elements that exists.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And in super-heavy elements, something extraordinary happens.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: The electrons in the outer shell move so fast that they enter what physicists call the relativistic

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: regime.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: They're traveling at a meaningful fraction of the speed of light.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And when something moves that fast, the universe starts playing by different rules.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: At those speeds, Einstein's physics takes over from Newton's.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: One consequence is that these screaming, curling outer electrons generate a powerful magnetic

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: field just from their own motion.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And that magnetic field slams into their own spin.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: This is a process called spin-orbit coupling.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And in bismuth, it's ferociously strong.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: So strong that bismuth's electrons become, in a sense, magnetically self-aware.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Generating an opposing field in response to anything applied to them from the outside.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: So that's why bismuth has anomalous diamagnetism.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: The electrons aren't just passive.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: They're pushing back.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: The most amazing thing is, leaning into it, putting all your force on that, nothing moves

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: at all.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: And when the reactor's off, you can easily slide it.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: This also makes bismuth a natural topological doping.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Meaning when you introduce it into certain crystalline materials, it induces what physicists call

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: topologically protected quantum states.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: These are electron states so geometrically locked into the structure of the material that

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: they can't be destroyed by disorder or impurities.

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: They are, in a very real sense, protected by the shape of reality itself.

text: Element 115, with the same five outer electrons as bismuth, would have dramatically stronger

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s

transcript_title: A Weekend With Bob Lazar Inside Area51s
text: Same column, same electrons.
